messages.content:
Act as a Beta Reder for this secon on a longer scifi story: The ‘Hall of Energy’ the oldest building on Earth was around some two hundred thousand years of age its vast interior was vast and dark, rows of Energy Beams hissed and cracked in their blue/white light interspaced white rings of energy pulsed up them at regular intervals. 
Circuits vast and ancient that looked like they were part of the stonework its self-carried information too complex to be understood by most, that is why the guardians where here to supervise this ancient yet vital technology.
Entering this intimidating place walk Fleet Captain McConnell less than five feet in she heard a voice call her name not localised but seeming to surround her.
‘Welcome Jade McConnell be mindful of where you are,’ the voice echoed, 
‘Quaint’ McConnell thought to herself
‘No,’ a similar voice echoed.
‘Who are you?’ McConnell’s voice echoed. 
Her question was met with silence.
‘I demand,’ McConnell insisted.
‘Mortals don’t demand here,’ the first voice echoed.
McConnell walked on the halls and pillars had metal braces aged and discoloured holding a torch set between every third was a large brazier, their flames caused the shadows to move, heat distortion rose.
‘What on Earth’ McConnell Thought
‘In dreams you have walked these halls, in those dreams you found your way;
May you tarry on and do so without delay?
Daughter of land so far, why is your sprite heavy and filled with dread?

Why must you enter here, and commune with those who are dead?

O daughter of a tribe whose count of days is few, why is you.
Filled with wow? 
Your soul is of fire, yet you chose to sleep in snow?
Languished long have you but yearn once more to be in space,
That histories writ recalls you took your place among your race.

Girl of promises where is your light that light you kept
Dimmed from you fellows less they see? ’ 
That you are more a stranger then they preventing yourself from being all 
-That you might be.

These halls ancient, echoes of voices, times long past
You are here to seek answers, to questions that.
-Must not be asked. 

Should you chose to ask those things then you must be given swift reply?
But be warned you may not like the truth, for the dead cannot lie.
Leave this place and do not return or carry on and lose part of yourself; but for now I say fare thee well and I shall see you at the end.

The voice said.

McConnell walked on her footsteps echoing in the space one person sounded like a hundred each echo lasted minutes before it decayed, even the sound of McConnell’s breathing and heartbeat echoed in this place. Her training as a girl prevented her panicking in this place.

The circuitry that ran all over the interior reconfigured itself constantly.

‘I’ve herd of structures like this but I didn’t know they were real’ flashed in McConnell’s mind.

‘Why do you doubt what you can see?’ A distant voice said.

Discussion on the Star Raptor Bridge
‘Well don’t just stand there complaining man speak up’ McConnell said to her first officer
The first officer was feeling insecure McConnell was starting to lose patience with him.
‘Say something or get off the bridge’ she said. 
‘The facts are clear Fleet Captain or at least as I see them, I think we should wait for the DSRRS to arrive and assess the situation’
‘You’re facts such as they are don’t add up to basic realities’
The Entire bridge crew looked in her direction McConnell knew she couldn’t hide certain facts any longer.
‘Everyone there are things that many of you may have heard as rumour I wish to lay things on the line now. As some of you know I have been working under a directive from both Stanner chairman Eric Hawley in conjunction with the Space Oversight Coucal to investigate the disperence of the Prince. During this time I learned that Adam Greene their chairman issued a directive to their fleet to cease all SRR operations for the Prince. ’
‘May I ask a question captain’ Donna said
‘You may’
‘Does that mean we are operating under Section 42(b) Subsection 12 of the Holman accords? ’ Donna said 
‘Yes Hazard Officer E’mer we are but primeraly we are here under Article 12 of the Space Medicine and Welfare Act and Article 7 of the Space Movement and Navigation Act ’
‘Article 7? Captain does that mean those two tugs, the medical ship and us is all there is? ’ Donna added 
‘Correct’ McConnell said
‘Anyone else?’
‘Captain’ Summua said
‘Go on’
‘Given the list angel of the Prince what will we do it the event that her power system cannot be brought back online? ’
McConnell nodded while remembering something from long ago.
‘Do you remember a ship called the Angus, about three years ago?’ McConnell said
‘I do and that’s what concerns me if you’ll beg pardon ma’am that was a section 21 rescue, two tugs pulling us behind providing propulsive force; the Angus was a smaller, lighter ship, not a CAG-Nomek 2032. Nether was the Angus at the degree of tilt the Prince is nor like the Prince was it in a dense asteroid field, captain if you order me in there that is an order I will refuse for the safety of this ship.” Summura said 
‘I wouldn’t ask you to take us in there; the prince can be pushed out by the tugs’ McConnell said
‘What about angel correction once you get an object the princes size in motion to tugs will find it impossible to correct lateral drift if its starts. Captain an object that size moving at that speed, the mass of the prince once converted into kinetic energy would do tremendous damage to her. ’ The first officer pointed out.
‘That is a very valid point there are two asteroids of concern that will need remote thrust packs of to move them out of the way, they will be under the command of AVIS ’McConnell said
‘You sound very sure Captain’ The First Officer Said.
McConnell looked at him and said.
‘What does that have to do with the mission? ’

The 'Hall of Energy,' the oldest building on Earth, was an ancient structure, approximately two hundred thousand years old. Its vast interior was shrouded in darkness, and rows of energy beams hissed and cracked in their blue-white light. Interspaced were white rings of energy pulsing at regular intervals. 

Circuits, vast and ancient, appeared to be part of the stonework itself. They carried information too complex to be understood by most. That's why the guardians were here - to supervise this ancient, yet vital technology.

As Fleet Captain McConnell entered this intimidating place, she heard a voice call her name. It was not localized but seemed to surround her.

'Welcome Jade McConnell, be mindful of where you are,' the voice echoed.

'Quaint,' McConnell thought to herself.

'No,' a similar voice echoed.

'Who are you?' McConnell's voice echoed.

Her question was met with silence.

'I demand,' McConnell insisted.

'Mortals don't demand here,' the first voice echoed.

McConnell continued walking through the halls and pillars, noticing metal braces aged and discolored between every third pillar. Each brace held a torch, causing shadows to dance and heat distortion to rise as she passed by.

'What on Earth?' McConnell thought.

A mysterious voice spoke, warning her of the consequences of seeking answers to forbidden questions within the hall. It told her truths she may not like, for the dead cannot lie and suggested that she leave or risk losing a part of herself.

McConnell pressed on, her footsteps echoing loudly and making her keenly aware of her breathing and heartbeat.  Her training as a girl prevented her from panicking in this unfamiliar environment. 

The circuitry that adorned the entire interior constantly reconfigured itself.

'I've heard of structures like this, but I didn't know they were real,' flashed in McConnell's mind.

'Why do you doubt what you can see?' A distant voice said.

---

Discussion on the Star Raptor Bridge:

'Well, don't just stand there complaining, man. Speak up,' McConnell said to her first officer.

The first officer was feeling insecure, and McConnell was starting to lose patience with him.

'Say something or get off the bridge,' she said.

The first officer explained his concerns about their rescue mission and the potential dangers of attempting to restore power to their ship, the Prince, particularly with a limited support team. He cited several examples of past missions with smaller ships and less hazardous circumstances and voiced his worry over the Prince's current position in a dense asteroid field.

McConnell acknowledged his concerns but reminded him of their responsibilities under various Space Medicine, Welfare Acts, and Navigation Acts. She had a plan to avoid putting the crew in unnecessary danger by using the tugs to push the Prince out of the asteroid field and correct its angle.

As the discussion continued, the crew raised valid concerns and suggestions. McConnell's confidence appeared unshakable until her first officer questioned her certainty.

'What does that have to do with the mission?' McConnell asked sharply, determined to navigate the challenges that lay ahead.
Act as Beta reader for the supplied text

The 'Hall of Energy,' the oldest building on Earth, was an ancient structure, approximately two hundred thousand years old. Its vast interior was shrouded in darkness, and rows of energy beams hissed and cracked in their blue-white light. Interspaced were white rings of energy pulsing at regular intervals.

Circuits, vast and ancient, appeared to be part of the stonework itself. They carried information too complex to be understood by most. That's why the guardians were here - to supervise this ancient yet vital technology.

As Fleet Captain McConnell entered this intimidating place, she heard a voice call her name. It was not localized but seemed to surround her.

'Welcome Jade McConnell, be mindful of where you are,' the voice echoed.

McConnell thought the place was mysterious, and continued walking, noticing metal braces aged and discolored between every third pillar. Each brace held a torch, causing shadows to dance and heat distortion to rise as she passed by.

A mysterious voice warned her of the consequences of seeking answers to forbidden questions within the hall and suggested that she leave or risk losing a part of herself.

McConnell pressed on, despite the echoing and mysterious voices. The circuitry that adorned the entire interior constantly reconfigured itself, making her doubt what she knew about such structures.

---

Discussion on the Star Raptor Bridge:

Fleet Captain McConnell urged her First Officer to share his concerns about their rescue mission. The officer was hesitant, but finally explained the potential dangers of trying to restore power to the stranded ship, the Prince, particularly with a limited support team and its location in a dense asteroid field.

McConnell acknowledged the concerns but cited their responsibilities under various Space Medicine, Welfare Acts, and Navigation Acts. She had a plan to push the Prince out of the asteroid field using tugs and correct its angle, without endangering the crew.

As the discussion continued, other crew members voiced their concerns and suggestions. Despite the growing apprehension, McConnell remained confident and focused on her mission to face the challenges ahead.
what improments should be made to make it more ingaging to a reader

To make the story more engaging to readers, consider the following improvements:

1. Character Development: Flesh out Captain McConnell and other characters by providing more background information, motives, and emotions. This will help readers connect with the characters and be more invested in their journey.

2. Pacing and Tension: Incorporate more moments of tension and suspense to keep readers on edge. For example, you can have Captain McConnell encounter more challenges in the Hall of Energy or face resistance from her crew while discussing the rescue mission.

3. Descriptive Language: Use more vivid and descriptive language to paint a clear picture in the reader's mind. Describe the environments, technology, and characters in more detail so readers can better envision the setting and feel more immersed in the story.

4. Dialogue: Make sure the dialogue between characters feels natural, flows smoothly, and advances the story. Avoid overly formal language, and consider using contractions to make the dialogue feel more conversational.

5. Conflict: Introduce more interpersonal and external conflicts to add depth to the story. For instance, you can explore disagreements among the crew members or have the mission challenged by unforeseen obstacles.

Here's an example of revised text with these improvements:

The 'Hall of Energy,' an ancient and enigmatic structure, was rumored to be the oldest building on Earth. Spanning over two hundred thousand years, its dark, expansive interior was brought to life by rows of hissing energy beams, emitting eerie blue-white light. Ancient circuits, embedded within the stonework, guarded secrets beyond human comprehension. Tasked with overseeing this vital yet mystifying technology, the guardians ensured no unauthorized access.

Fleet Captain Jade McConnell, a resilient and experienced leader, cautiously stepped into the intimidating hall. She felt a sudden chill as a disembodied voice called her name, not localized but seemingly surrounding her. Greeting her ominously, the voice ignited an uneasy curiosity within her.

---

Later, in the Star Raptor Bridge, McConnell urged her First Officer to share his concerns about their high-stakes rescue mission. He seemed hesitant, but eventually elaborated on the potential dangers of attempting to restore power to the stranded ship, the Prince. The limited support team, coupled with their location in a dense and treacherous asteroid field, added to the mission's uncertainty.

Acknowledging these concerns, McConnell drew upon her years of experience and wisdom and cited their responsibilities under various Space Medicine, Welfare Acts, and Navigation Acts. Despite the anxious murmurs from her crew, she confidently outlined her plan: using tugs to safely guide the Prince out of the asteroid field while risking as few lives as possible.

Tension filled the room as crew members argued and questioned their captain's decision. However, Captain McConnell's unwavering determination propelled her and her team forward, knowing that the fate of the Prince and its crew rested on their shoulders.
are contractions aceptible in a profesonal setting

Yes, contractions are generally acceptable in a professional setting. They make the language more conversational, approachable, and easier to read. However, the appropriateness of contractions may depend on the specific context, audience, and formality level of the situation.

In informal professional situations or communications with colleagues, contractions are usually considered appropriate. Formal documents, like legal papers, scientific research papers, or high-level reports, may require more formal language, where contractions might be avoided.

When in doubt, consider your audience and the purpose of your communication to determine whether contractions are appropriate for the specific professional setting.
how based on the supplied text might more tesion be added

To add more tension to the supplied text, you can introduce additional challenges and conflicts that the characters must face, both in the Hall of Energy and during the mission discussion. Here's a revised version with added tension:

Fleet Captain Jade McConnell, a resilient and experienced leader, cautiously stepped into the intimidating Hall of Energy. She felt a sudden chill as a disembodied voice called her name, not localized but seemingly coming from all directions. Greeting her ominously, the voice ignited an uneasy curiosity within her. As Jade ventured deeper into the ancient hall, she noticed the energy beams growing more unstable and occasionally malfunctioning. The sense of danger grew with each step, while her determination to uncover the hall's secrets intensified.

---

Later, in the Star Raptor Bridge, Captain McConnell urged her hesitant First Officer to share his concerns about their high-stakes rescue mission. As his fears regarding the stranded ship's location within the treacherous asteroid field came to light, the crew watched with bated breath, their anxiety palpable. Jade's calm demeanor began to show cracks when additional crew members voiced their own concerns, pleading for a less risky course of action.

Suddenly, an alarm blared, indicating an urgent problem with the Prince. The room filled with tension as McConnell was forced to make a decision under pressure, weighing the risks against the potential losses. The First Officer challenged her plan, proposing an alternative approach, while the rest of the crew watched silently, the air thick with anticipation.

With each passing moment, the crew's doubts grew, and Captain McConnell's unwavering determination was put to the test. Ultimately, she chose to proceed with her original plan, knowing that the fate of the Prince and its crew depended on her ability to navigate the manifold dangers they were about to face.

By introducing additional challenges, emphasizing the stakes, and presenting conflicts among the crew, the revised text creates a more tense and engaging atmosphere for the reader.

there is more in the Hall of Energy: 
 No one remembered anymore who had built the Hall of Power or why. Filled with echoes of the past and answers to questions not yet asked, Captain Jade McConnell needed to be here, though she'd been cautioned not to come. '
 Welcome, a voice echoed, seeming to envelop her. McConnell was aware of such games; she'd engaged in them in Africa as a girl. 'Guardians, I am a knowledge seeker. I wish your help, she said, but the voice replied in the wind.'
 I cannot help you; I'm a guide to the past.
Though you may wish me to' the voice grew stronger still. She couldn't help but hear the voice of the past: For there I hear you will succeed or fail'.
 The girl nodded and walked past a sign on the way in, stating, 'Don't you even think about it!' In faded letters.
She felt a chill go down her back and a sense of dread. She wanted no part in it, no part in such ancient things. But she had been here before.
She grasped what to do. She made her way to the centre of the circular room and looked around.
The light was dim, though the room was vast. Ahead, she saw only blank stones. She felt it in her back. There is no light there.
Something about it made her stop.
She felt around for a light.
Nothing. She looked back towards the centre of the room.
There, she saw a small blue orb. The only source of light in the room.
'Blue light," she thought, "I've seen blue light before."
She moved towards the orb. She was aware of the ground turning to water and rising to meet her.
In a flash, she was in a room. She stood looking at the door.
She smiled. She closed her eyes and was back in the hall, facing the empty orb. She looked up at the sky. It had changed.
 I was looking for something.
"I see you." And I see you find it.
‘She saw a flash of light.
'We are the same,' she replied.
'As are we all,' the voice said. 'We are nothing. We are,' said another voice.
That makes us powerful,' the first voice answered. 'We have much to do,' the first voice said. McConnell opened her eyes and looked around her.
It was still the same hall, and she was still on the same planet. 'I wish to go back to my world,' she thought. She remembered the first part of the riddle.
 'No one has seen it.' She took a deep breath and closed her eyes. 'No,' she whispered, You've been trying to tell me something for weeks, and now you've come to me. But no one sees it. No one has seen it.
That's the riddle of the hall, she thought. She opened her eyes and closed them again. She'd know the answer when she saw it.
The door behind her was opening. 'No,' she said, don't open it' though she knew it was coming.
 She advanced onward, explored the room, and comprehended what was behind the door. The light from the room behind her fell on it.
 She turned and walked through the door. The airlock was spinning its glassy red light.
A figure floated in the air, and she didn't know what it was.
"Are you a part of this?" The dark man answered.
'I have been for a long time now," it said.
 'For which I'm very sorry' McConnell said.
The dark man laughed. 'It is done,' the dark man said.
McConnell nodded, and they all dissolved in a blue light.
'I remember this place," Jade thought.
"No," she said, "I remember everything.'
She looked back at the door and realised she was looking at the end of her life and that she had been coming here.
She closed her eyes, and a flash of blue light filled the room.
She was standing in a green and red cave. A few feet in front of her was a pool of water that flowed into the red and green cave walls.
McConnell knew this cave. It was the only place she'd been able to find to stop the dreams. But it wasn't helping now.
 It's not working," she thought. She thought that it wasn't working when she came in here before.
 What's changed?'  She didn't know the answer to that question, and, for once, she wasn't afraid.
 She wasn't angry or sad. She felt lonely.
Why won't it stop? She thought of the dark man and the riddle.
It's been written,' she thought. 'I have written it for a reason. There was something about the way things were. It was all true.
 If he was a part of it, maybe so was everyone else.
‘She was aware of the past and the things that had happened. ' But it wasn't the past,' she thought. It was all in the past.
How could she change from the past? She had come here to save the past.' She shook her head and remembered the words of Captain Sacket.
"There are many ways to interpret a riddle, captain."
"The first part of the riddle had been, 'What has never been seen?'"
 She thought of what she saw every night.

The Hall of Power, an ancient and enigmatic structure, held echoes of the past and answers to questions not yet asked. No one remembered who had built it or why, but Fleet Captain Jade McConnell knew she needed to be there, despite the warnings.

Upon entering the hall, a ghostly voice greeted her. Not easily intimidated, Captain McConnell declared herself a knowledge seeker, requesting the voice's assistance. The voice, claiming to be a guide to the past, responded cryptically, alluding to a crucial role McConnell would play - either to succeed or fail.

As McConnell ventured further, drawn in by a small blue orb, she navigated through illusions and challenges. The ground transformed into water, she found herself in different rooms, and soon faced the mysterious "dark man" to unlock the riddle. McConnell's thoughts raced, trying to decipher cryptic messages and visions of the past.

With each clue and illusion, she felt a growing sense of dread and loneliness - her past and future intertwined, creating a sense of urgency. As McConnell explored the unknown, the tension built, drawing her closer to the resolution of the riddle.

Returning to the concept of the unseen, McConnell considered Captain Sacket's advice - there are many ways to interpret a riddle. Facing the unknown, she understood that her perspective on the past could change. Armed with this knowledge, Captain McConnell's journey in the Hall of Power intensified, with the riddle's resolution shaping her destiny.

By incorporating additional twists and challenges, emphasizing the emotional weight and turmoil within the protagonist, and setting a greater sense of urgency, this revised text builds tension, making the story more engaging to the reader.
What is missing or potenual plot hole that might cuase confusion

There are a few potential plot holes or confusing elements in the text that may need to be addressed:

1. The Hall of Power's purpose: The purpose of the hall and the nature of the unseen guiding force in the hall are not explicitly stated, which may leave readers questioning the significance of this location.

2. McConnell's motivation: McConnell is seeking knowledge from the Hall of Power, but it is unclear what specific knowledge she is searching for and why she needs it. Providing a clearer motivation for her journey into the hall would make her actions more understandable to readers.

3. The dark man: The dark man's identity, purpose, and connection to McConnell are vague, which may create confusion. Expanding on this character's role in the story could provide context for his presence and his involvement in the riddle.

4. The riddle itself: The riddle and its importance are introduced, but the consequences or significance of solving it are not clearly presented. Providing a resolution to the riddle and linking it to McConnell's motivation would clarify its role within the story.

5. Captain Sacket: Captain Sacket is briefly mentioned, but the connection to McConnell and how the advice about riddles affects her journey are not explicitly discussed. Expanding on their relationship and Sacket's relevance to the story would provide readers with a better understanding of the context.

Addressing these potential issues will create a more cohesive story and reduce confusion for readers.
Diving into the subtext of the supplied text what others might there be

There are a few possible subtexts or underlying themes within the supplied text:

1. The journey of self-discovery: Captain McConnell's venture into the Hall of Power can be interpreted as a metaphor for her personal journey, confronting her past, her fears, and discovering her true self and purpose.

2. The nature of time and memory: By weaving between past, present, and future, the story explores the nonlinear and often unpredictable nature of time and memory. This theme encourages readers to reflect upon their own experiences and the impact that memories have on their decisions and actions.

3. The power of perception: The illusions and challenges that McConnell faces in the Hall of Power serve as reminders that reality can be subjective. This theme suggests that one's outlook and interpretation of events can shape their journey and outcome.

4. Overcoming fear and challenges: Captain McConnell demonstrates resilience and determination despite the unknown dangers surrounding her. This highlights the importance of confronting one's fears, embracing challenges, and persevering even when faced with adversity.

5. The interconnectedness of people and events: By introducing the dark man and Captain Sacket and their respective roles in the story, the text emphasizes that the fates of different characters may be interconnected. This theme suggests that the consequences of one's actions can reverberate and impact the lives of others in different ways.

Analyzing the subtext within the supplied text can reveal underlying themes that enrich the storyline and provide deeper meaning for readers.
Given what you have just said have their been any novels within the scifi genre that have explored these theams

Yes, there have been several science fiction novels that explore the themes discussed. Here are a few examples:

1. "Dune" by Frank Herbert: This classic science fiction novel also delves into the themes of self-discovery, personal growth, and the interconnectedness of people and events. The protagonist, Paul Atreides, navigates a complex sociopolitical environment while discovering his own inner power.

2. "Slaughterhouse-Five" by Kurt Vonnegut: This novel explores themes of time, memory, and the impact of past experiences on the protagonist, Billy Pilgrim. The story is driven by a nonlinear timeline and examines the nature of time and its effect on human experiences.

3. "The Left Hand of Darkness" by Ursula K. Le Guin: This novel delves into themes of perception, understanding, and overcoming challenges through the protagonist's experiences on an alien planet inhabited by a genderless society. The story explores how different perspectives shape an individual's perception of reality.

4. "Hyperion" by Dan Simmons: This science fiction novel uses multiple interconnected narratives to examine the interconnectedness of people and events. The story is focused on seven characters, each with their own tale, highlighting the impact of one's actions on others and the collective human experience.

5. "The Time Traveler's Wife" by Audrey Niffenegger: Although not a traditional sci-fi novel, this story revolves around time travel. The theme of time and memory plays a significant role as readers witness the relationship between Henry, a time traveler, and Clare, his wife, unfold in a non-linear fashion.

These novels have successfully explored similar themes and convey deeper messages while providing engaging storylines for readers within the science fiction genre.
Can Vageness be used to create mystery or do readers find it offputting

Vagueness can indeed be used to create mystery and intrigue in a story, encouraging readers to keep turning the pages for further information. By intentionally withholding specific details or leaving certain elements open to interpretation, writers can pique readers' curiosity and stimulate their imagination.

However, striking the right balance is essential. If the narrative becomes too vague or confusing, readers may find it off-putting and lose interest in the story. Here are a few tips to use vagueness effectively while maintaining a compelling narrative:

1. Introduce sufficient clarity: Make sure there's enough information provided for readers to follow the plot and understand the key elements, even if some details remain ambiguous.

2. Limit vague elements: Focus on using vagueness intentionally for specific plot points or characters to create a sense of mystery. Avoid applying it to the entire story, as this may lead to confusion and disinterest.

3. Provide resolution: While leaving some aspects open to interpretation can add depth and intrigue, it's generally important to resolve the main conflicts and mysteries by the end of the story to create a satisfying reading experience.

4. Develop engaging characters: If your story is intentionally vague, make sure your characters are well-developed and relatable to keep readers invested despite the narrative uncertainty.

5. Let the context guide: In genres like mystery, suspense, or psychological thrillers, readers may expect an element of vagueness, while in other genres, they may find it more surprising. Use vagueness strategically according to your genre and story context.

When used purposefully and in moderation, vagueness can enhance the suspense, mystery, and intrigue in a story, while still providing readers with an engaging and satisfying experience.
